title: Lists and dicts — the bones of every API
blurb: Every JSON response you've ever copied out of ChatGPT or a REST API is some mix of two things: lists and dicts. Read them on sight.

lessons.title: Lists, dicts, and the shape of an API response

steps.body:
# Every API response is some mix of two things

Every JSON blob you've copied out of ChatGPT, every REST response, every
config file Cursor wrote you — it's all the same two shapes nested inside
each other:

- **List** — `[a, b, c]` — ordered. Get an item by position: `tags[0]`.
- **Dict** — `{"name": "Maya"}` — labeled. Get a value by name: `user["name"]`.

The code on the right is a typical user object — a dict that contains a
list. Hit **Run**. Notice line 6 reaches into the list *inside* the dict
with `user["tags"][0]`. That stacking is what every API response looks like.

hint.body: *Map each X to Y* is the giveaway phrase. You need labels.

steps.prompt: Four shapes. Which one would Cursor reach for if you asked it to *map each user to their score*?

options.label: `a` — the list
options.explain: A list keeps order but has no labels. You can't ask it for `a["maya"]`.

options.label: `c` — the tuple
options.explain: A tuple is like a list but you can't change it. Still no labels.

options.label: `d` — the set
options.explain: A set is unordered, no duplicates, no labels. Useful, but not for mapping users to scores.

steps.body:
# Indexing starts at zero, and `-1` is the last item

Two rules you'll trip over once and never again:

- **Lists count from `0`**, not `1`. The first item is `pets[0]`.
- **Negative numbers count from the end.** `pets[-1]` is the last item,
  no matter how long the list is.

Run the code. The script prints `luna`, `moose`, `biscuit`, then `biscuit`
again — because `[-1]` and `[2]` happen to point at the same spot in this
3-item list.

This is one of the top off-by-one bugs AI ships: it'll write `for i in range(1, len(items))` when it meant `range(len(items))`, skipping the first item silently.

hint.body: Read it left to right. `users[1]` is the second user. Then `["name"]` reaches into that user's dict.
`users[1]` is `{"name": "marcus", "score": 78}`. Then `["name"]` is `marcus`.

steps.prompt: This is the shape every API hands back: a list of dicts. What does line 5 print?

hint.body: Dict keys go inside square brackets. The key here is the string `"email"`.
`user["email"]` — quotes around the key, because it's a string.

steps.prompt:
Cursor wrote a quick lookup of a user's email. It defined the dict on line 1
and tried to reach for the email on line 2, but it left a blank where the
key should go. Fill it in.
```python
user = {"name": "{{user.name}}", "email": "you@example.com", "score": 92}
print(user[___])
```
The script should print the email address.

hint.body: `KeyError` means the dict has no key by that name. Look at line 1 — what's the actual key?
The key on line 1 is `"name"`, not `"username"`. Change the lookup on line 2 to match.

steps.prompt:
Claude generated this and it crashes with a `KeyError: 'username'`. That's the
error Python throws when you ask a dict for a key it doesn't have.

Fix the code so it prints `hi, maya`.

hint.body: Lists count from zero. Which index is the first item?
Change `users[1]` to `users[0]` on line 6.

steps.prompt:
Cursor wrote a script to print the first user's name. It uses `users[1]`, which
is wrong — that's the *second* user. The output is `marcus` and it should be `maya`.

Fix line 6 so the script prints `first user: maya`.

hint.body: `.append` is a method you call on the list itself. Two lines: one append, one print.
`pets.append("biscuit")` then `print(pets)`.

steps.prompt:
Start with the list `pets = ["luna", "moose"]`. Add `"biscuit"` to the end of
it using the `.append` method, then print the whole list.

Expected output:
```
['luna', 'moose', 'biscuit']
```
steps.starter:
pets = ["luna", "moose"]
# add "biscuit" to the end:

# print the list:

hint.body: Two reaches stacked. First the index of the second user, then the `score` key.
`users[1]["score"]` is `78`. Wrap that in an f-string: `print(f"second user score: {users[1]['score']}")`.

steps.prompt:
One last thing. AI handed back a list of two users from a fake API. Your job:
print the second user's score.

The starter has the data. Finish line 6 so the script prints `second user score: 78`.
steps.starter:
users = [
    {"name": "maya", "score": 92},
    {"name": "marcus", "score": 78},
]
# your code on the next line — print the second user's score using an f-string:

lessons.title: The one-liner Python writes when it's showing off

steps.body:
# The one-liner AI loves

When AI generates a "transform every item in this list" snippet, it almost
never writes the four-line `for` loop. It writes a **list comprehension** —
the same idea collapsed onto one line.

The shape on the right shows both forms doing the same job. Read them as:

- *"a new list, where each item is `p * 1.1`, for every `p` in `prices`"*

Run the code. Both lines print the same list. The comprehension is what
you'll see in 90% of AI-written Python — `[expression for item in iterable]`.

The trap: comprehensions look dense at first. The trick is reading them
**left to right** as plain English, not as code.
steps.code:
prices = [10, 20, 30]

# the long way
with_tax = []
for p in prices:
    with_tax.append(p * 1.1)

# the same thing as a comprehension
with_tax_short = [p * 1.1 for p in prices]

print(with_tax)
print(with_tax_short)

hint.body: Square brackets, expression first, then `for item in iterable`. That's the whole pattern.
Read it left to right: *make a list of `name.upper()`, for every `name` in `names`*. Option A is the only valid Python.

steps.prompt:
You want a new list with every name in upper case. Four candidates from
Cursor. Only one is a valid Python list comprehension. Which one?

options.label: `[for name in names: name.upper()]`
options.explain: The expression goes first, then the `for`. No colon.

options.label: `{name.upper() for name in names}`
options.explain: Curly braces produce a set, not a list. Different shape, different behavior.

options.label: `[name.upper() in names]`
options.explain: Missing the `for name`. Without it, Python doesn't know what to iterate over.

steps.body:
# Comprehensions can filter, too

Tack an `if` onto the end and the comprehension only keeps the items that
pass the test.

The pattern grows by one piece:

```
[expression for item in iterable if condition]
```

Read it as: *"a new list, where each item is `expression`, for every `item`
in `iterable`, but only when `condition` is true."*

Run the code on the right.

- `passing` keeps only scores ≥ 70 → `[91, 72, 88]`
- `doubled` does the same filter, then doubles each one → `[182, 144, 176]`

This is the Swiss-army knife AI reaches for whenever the prompt has the
words *"only the ones that..."* in it.
steps.code:
scores = [55, 91, 72, 40, 88, 65]

# keep only the passing scores
passing = [s for s in scores if s >= 70]

# double every score, but only the passing ones
doubled = [s * 2 for s in scores if s >= 70]

print(passing)
print(doubled)

hint.body: The `if n % 2 == 0` keeps only even numbers. Then each survivor gets squared.
Even numbers in `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` are `2` and `4`. Squared: `4` and `16`. Output is the list `[4, 16]`.

steps.code:
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
result = [n * n for n in numbers if n % 2 == 0]
print(result)
steps.prompt:
Read the comprehension left to right. Filter first, then transform.
What does this print?

hint.body: Every list comprehension needs the keyword that introduces the loop variable.
It's `for`. The shape is `[expression for item in iterable]`.

steps.prompt:
Cursor wrote a comprehension that should walk through `words` and produce
a list of their lengths. The keyword on line 2 is missing. Fill it in.
```python
words = ["hi", "hello", "hey"]
lengths = [len(w) ___ w in words]
print(lengths)
```

hint.body: The expression always goes first, then `for item in iterable`. Not the other way around.
Change line 2 to `doubled = [n * 2 for n in numbers]`. Expression first, loop clause second.

steps.brokenCode:
numbers = [1, 2, 3]
doubled = [for n in numbers n * 2]
print(doubled)
steps.prompt:
Claude generated this and it crashes with a `SyntaxError`. The pieces of the
comprehension are in the wrong order — the `for` clause came first instead
of the expression. Reorder line 2 so the script prints `[2, 4, 6]`.

hint.body: An `if` at the *end* of a comprehension filters items out. An `if/else` *inside* the expression replaces them. You want the filter.
Change line 2 to `long_names = [n for n in names if len(n) > 3]`. Move the `if` to the end and drop the `else` branch.

steps.brokenCode:
names = ["alex", "bo", "jordan", "sam"]
long_names = [n if len(n) > 3 else "-" for n in names]
print(long_names)
steps.prompt:
Cursor wanted to keep only names longer than 3 characters. Instead of
filtering them out, it used an `if/else` *inside* the expression, so short
names got replaced with the string `"-"` instead of removed.

Output is currently `['alex', '-', 'jordan', '-']`. Fix line 2 so the
output is `['alex', 'jordan']` — short names removed entirely.

hint.body: Two pieces. The comprehension `[p for p in prices if p > 15]`. Then a `print` of whatever you assigned it to.
`expensive = [p for p in prices if p > 15]` then `print(expensive)`.

steps.prompt:
Write a list comprehension that takes the list `prices = [12, 50, 8, 99, 30]`
and produces a new list containing only the prices over `15`. Print the
result.

Expected output:
```
[50, 99, 30]
```
steps.solution:
prices = [12, 50, 8, 99, 30]
expensive = [p for p in prices if p > 15]
print(expensive)

hint.body: Read it as a sentence: *the name of each user, for every user, if the user's age is at least 18.* That's the comprehension.
`adult_names = [u["name"] for u in users if u["age"] >= 18]`. Replace the `pass`.

steps.prompt:
Last one. Cursor wrote a snippet to take a list of user dicts and pull out
just the names of users whose age is at least 18. The comprehension is
missing — only the `pass` placeholder is there.

Replace the `pass` line with a single comprehension so the script prints
`['alex', 'jordan']`.
steps.starter:
users = [
    {"name": "alex", "age": 34},
    {"name": "bo", "age": 12},
    {"name": "jordan", "age": 41},
    {"name": "sam", "age": 9},
]

# build a list of names where age >= 18
adult_names = pass

print(adult_names)
steps.solution:
users = [
    {"name": "alex", "age": 34},
    {"name": "bo", "age": 12},
    {"name": "jordan", "age": 41},
    {"name": "sam", "age": 9},
]

adult_names = [u["name"] for u in users if u["age"] >= 18]

print(adult_names)
